strengths: Structured claim-level memory with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges addresses a critical gap in existing agent memory systems
Paired set-inclusion gains on abstention and single-session-user slices demonstrate clear truth-maintenance benefits
Efficiency improvements over A-Mem (-62.1% tokens) show practical deployment potential
Ablation isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains, confirming mechanism validity
weaknesses: Overall accuracy not statistically different from baseline despite targeted improvements
Regressions on assistant-authored, preference, and multi-session slices reveal structural limitations
Intent classifier's low recall on multi-session/knowledge-update questions limits router effectiveness
No explicit handling of multi-claim composition in reader prompt
required_revisions: Improve assistant-side claim extraction density to address SSA/SSP regressions
Enhance intent classifier recall on multi-session and knowledge-update slices
Implement multi-claim reasoning in reader prompt for cross-session aggregation
Clarify how validity intervals are updated for overlapping claims without supersede edges

strengths: Paired set-inclusion gains on abstention and SSU slices with statistical tests
Ablation isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of truth-maintenance gains
Efficiency improvements over A-Mem with -62.1% token cost
Router composition preserves Flat RAG's overall accuracy while retaining truth-maintenance behaviors
weaknesses: Small slice sizes (n=9 for abstention) limit statistical power
Regressions on SSA, SSP, and MS slices not fully mitigated
One-sided McNemar p-values for abstention (p=0.0625) approach significance thresholds
Oracle router upper bound requires ground-truth labels, not deployable
required_revisions: Justify one-sided vs two-sided McNemar test choices in the methodology
Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's h) alongside p-values for slice comparisons
Provide more details on how the oracle router is constructed
Include analysis of Type I/II error rates for the LLM intent classifier in Appendix~\ref{app:intent}

strengths: Structured claim schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges addresses temporal consistency gaps in existing memory systems
Paired set-inclusion wins on abstention and single-session-user slices demonstrate clear gains where truth maintenance matters
Efficiency metrics show significant token reduction over A-Mem (-62.1%) while maintaining retrieval quality
Ablation isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains, confirming its necessity
weaknesses: Regressions on assistant-authored, preference, and multi-session slices suggest structural limitations in claim-as-atom abstraction
Intent classifier for TTMG+R-LLM has low recall on multi-session (42.5%) and knowledge-update (39.1%) questions
Overall accuracy parity with Flat RAG requires post-hoc router composition, not the core TTMG mechanism
No explicit handling of multi-claim composition in reader prompts, limiting performance on aggregation tasks
required_revisions: Improve assistant-authored claim extraction density to address F1 failure mode
Implement multi-claim reasoning in reader context for multi-session questions (F3)
Enhance intent classifier recall on knowledge-update and multi-session slices
Clarify trade-offs between claim-level abstraction and raw-text retrieval in system design discussion

strengths: Claim-level schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges is novel for agent memory
Paired set-inclusion wins on abstention (+44.4 pp) and single-session-user (+9.5 pp) slices
Router composition recovers Flat RAG overall accuracy while preserving truth-maintenance behaviour
Lower token cost (-62.1 %) and latency vs A-Mem
Ablation shows LLM linker is causal driver of gains
weaknesses: Overall accuracy not statistically better than Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %, p=0.21)
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS slices due to over-abstention and narrow claim context
Intent classifier recall low on multi-session (42.5 %) and knowledge-update (39.1 %)
Small abstention slice (n=9) yields McNemar p=0.0625, only trend-level significance
Oracle upper bound 82 % shows large complementary gap not closed by simple router
required_revisions: Provide exact p-values for all McNemar tests (some only report 0.0625)
Clarify why 150-question stratified slice is sufficient power for the claimed gains
Release full 500-question LongMemEval-S results to assess slice stability
Explain why Flat RAG baseline does not use same session-batched writer as TTMG
Add error bars or CIs for per-slice accuracies, not just overall

strengths: Novel claim-level memory schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges
Paired set-inclusion wins on abstention (+44.4 pp) and single-session-user (+9.5 pp) slices
Thorough ablation showing the LLM linker is causally necessary (-2.6 pp overall, -22 pp ABS)
Reproducible release: code, prompts, 150-question seed, raw per-question outputs, McNemar script
Lower token cost than A-Mem (-62 %) while retaining competitive accuracy when composed with Flat RAG
weaknesses: Only 9 abstention questions → McNemar p=0.0625 one-sided is not significant at α=0.05
Overall accuracy drop 68→61 % (n=150) is not significant (p=0.21) but still a practical regression
Intent classifier recall on multi-session only 42.5 %, leaving 15 pp on the table vs oracle
Writer under-extracts assistant claims (7/7 SSA errors) and multi-claim aggregations (8/8 MS)
No cross-dataset transfer: results reported only on one 150-q stratified slice of LongMemEval-S
required_revisions: Run power analysis and increase abstention n to ≥20 to claim significance at α=0.05
Add Bonferroni or FDR correction for the six slice-wise McNemar tests to avoid multiplicity
Evaluate on the full LongMemEval-S 500-q set or on LoCoMo/MemoryAgentBench to show generalisation
Report confidence intervals and discordant counts for every slice (missing for SSA/SSP/MS)
Release the exact 150-q IDs and the LLM judge prompts to let reviewers replicate the scorer

strengths: Claim-level schema with validity intervals and explicit contradict/supersede edges is a clean abstraction for truth maintenance
Session-batched writer + lexical gate keeps LLM linker off the hot path; 62 % token reduction vs A-Mem
Paired set-inclusion wins on abstention (+44 pp) and single-session-user (+9 pp) with small N are concrete
Router composition recovers Flat-RAG overall accuracy while preserving truth behaviour; oracle 82 % shows complementarity
Full reproducibility package: prompts, code, seeds, per-question JSON released
weaknesses: N=150 stratified slice is tiny; 9 abstention questions drive the headline 44 pp gain – too few to trust
Intent classifier recall 42 % on multi-session leaves 15 pp on the table vs oracle; real router still brittle
Writer under-extracts assistant claims (7/7 SSA failures); claim atom too narrow for preference & aggregation tasks
No throughput numbers at scale (only 150 Q latency); graph update cost & contention under concurrent writers unknown
Hard-edge threshold τ_hard=0.7, k_keep=3, bypass=0.85 chosen once; no sensitivity sweep or ablation on these
required_revisions: Run on full LongMemEval-S 500-Q set and report macro-averaged per-type CIs; abstention slice still <30 Q so combine with LoCoMo/MemoryAgentBench abstention probes
Add micro-benchmark: ingest 10 k sessions with 10 concurrent writers, measure linker latency p95 and write amplification (edges per claim)
Sweep τ_hard ∈ {0.5,0.6,0.7,0.8} and k_keep ∈ {2,3,5} on 20 % held-out slice; report slice accuracy vs token cost Pareto
Ablation: compare against claim schema *without* validity interval (i.e. triples only) to isolate temporal vs structural benefit
Release throughput script: tokens/second and queries/second vs haystack size (1,10,50 sessions) on a single A100

strengths: Novel application of classical truth maintenance (TMS, AGM) to LLM agent memory with explicit contradict/supersede edges
Sound experimental methodology with paired McNemar tests, bootstrap CIs, and proper ablation study
Clear failure mode taxonomy (F1-F3) honestly reporting regressions on SSA, SSP, MS slices
Meaningful efficiency gains: 62% token reduction vs A-Mem baseline
Well-written with comprehensive appendix including prompts, hyperparameters, and reproducibility statement
weaknesses: Headline abstation result (n=9) and SSU result (n=21) have very small sample sizes; McNemar p=0.0625 is borderline at best
Overall accuracy of TTMG (61.3%) is numerically lower than Flat RAG (68.0%), though not statistically significant
System regresses on three slices (SSA, SSP, MS); router composition is essentially a workaround to recover baseline performance
Intent classifier has poor recall on key classes (42.5% MS, 39.1% KU, 22.2% SSP), limiting router effectiveness
A-Mem baseline in Table 1 has missing entries (--), making full comparison difficult
required_revisions: Increase sample size for abstention and SSU slices to provide statistically robust evidence for headline claims
Fill in missing A-Mem numbers in Table 1 or explain why they cannot be computed
Provide deeper analysis of whether regressions are fundamental to claim-level abstraction or addressable via method extensions
Report effect sizes alongside p-values for all statistical tests
Consider evaluating on full LongMemEval-S (500 questions) rather than 150-question slice to strengthen empirical claims

strengths: Fair baseline comparison: identical embedding model, reader, and retrieval budget across all systems
Appropriate statistical testing: paired McNemar tests with exact p-values, 95% bootstrap CIs
Ablation study isolates LLM linker's causal contribution with drops across all slices
Detailed failure mode taxonomy (F1-F3) with specific counts and structural explanations
Efficiency analysis shows -62.1% token reduction vs A-Mem with latency improvements
Practical deployment path via inference-time routers that don't require benchmark labels
Reproducibility: Appendix includes prompts, hyperparameters, seed values, promises code release
weaknesses: Very small sample sizes for headline claims: abstention n=9, SSU n=21
Abstention result marginally non-significant: one-sided p=0.0625, two-sided p=0.125
SSU result not statistically significant: McNemar p=0.50 despite claimed 'win'
Overall accuracy regression: TTMG 61.3% vs Flat RAG 68.0% (though p=0.21)
A-Mem baseline incomplete: Table 1 shows '--' for SSA, SSP, SSU slices
Single random seed (seed=0): no robustness check across multiple runs
Limited ablation scope: only --contradict tested, not gate/writer/parser/k-value
Router composition loses abstention benefit: routers' ABS accuracy equals Flat's, not TTMG's
Intent classifier weak: 64.7% overall, 42.5% MS recall, 39.1% KU recall
required_revisions: Report results across multiple random seeds (e.g., 5 seeds) to demonstrate robustness
Complete A-Mem baseline results for all slices or explain why they are missing
Add per-slice confidence intervals or standard errors, not just overall bootstrap CIs
Be more conservative in statistical claims: abstention p=0.0625 is marginal, SSU p=0.50 is not a win
Add ablations for: subject-predicate gate, session-batched writer, temporal parser, k values
Address router abstention loss: propose concrete mechanism to preserve TTMG's abstention behavior in composition
Consider larger sample size for abstention slice or pool with additional abstention probes

strengths: Significant cost reduction: 13,603 tokens/question vs 35,904 for A-Mem (-62.1%)
Latency improvement: 85.0s vs 117.1s for A-Mem
Session-batched writing (1 LLM call/session vs per-turn) is a practical optimization
Cheap subject-predicate gate reduces unnecessary linker LLM calls
Inference-time routers (TTMG-Abstain, TTMG+R-LLM) are deployable without benchmark labels
Clean ablation showing linker is causally necessary for gains
Paired McNemar tests on same questions, not different samples
Comprehensive appendix with prompts, hyperparameters, reproducibility statement
weaknesses: Standalone TTMG regresses on overall accuracy: 61.3% vs Flat RAG's 68.0%
Gains on very small slices: n=9 abstention, n=21 SSU - underpowered for strong claims
Needs router composition to match baseline - standalone system is weaker
Intent classifier weak: 64.7% overall, 42.5% recall on multi-session
Writer entity gap: assistant-authored content poorly extracted (7/7 SSA failures)
No real-world workload testing - only benchmark evaluation
Limited scale: 150 questions, max 3 sessions per question
Multiple LLM calls (writer, linker, parser, reader) increase integration complexity and failure modes
Oracle gap (82% vs 66-67%) shows substantial unexploited complementarity
required_revisions: Report results on full LongMemEval-S (500 questions) rather than 150-question slice
Add statistical power analysis for small slices (n=9, n=21)
Test with more sessions per question to evaluate scalability
Report failure rate and latency distribution (not just mean) for LLM calls
Address writer entity gap for assistant-authored content before claiming practical deployability
